front: Poem: opens 'I have gone out, a possessed witch, haunting the black air'; three stanzas each end with the refrain 'I have been her kind'
Poem: 'a woman like that is not a woman, quite'; compares the speaker to a witch, a crazy woman in a cave, and a persecuted woman
Poem: elegy for the poet's father, concluding 'Whether you are pretty or not, I outlive you, / bend down my strange face to yours and forgive you'; title from Macduff's reaction in Macbeth
Poem: catalogs the father's possessions: boxes of pictures, a five-year diary the mother kept for three years, an English Ford
Poem: 'suicides have a special language. Like carpenters they want to know which tools. They never ask why build'
Poem: death leaves 'the page of the book carelessly open,' 'the phone off the hook,' and 'the love, whatever it was, an infection'
Poem: the first poem observes 'God was there like an island' and repeats 'I am rowing, I am rowing'; the speaker is 'stamped out like a Plymouth fender into this world'
back: The Awful Rowing Toward God (Anne Sexton)

front: Poem: the final poem 'The Rowing Endeth' describes the speaker getting a royal flush but losing to God's five aces in poker upon arriving at 'the island called God'
back: The Awful Rowing Toward God (Anne Sexton)

front: Poem: Pulitzer-winning collection titled from Saul Bellow's Herzog; includes 'Sylvia's Death' and a poem about menstruating at age forty
Poem: 'Sylvia, Sylvia, where did you go after you wrote me from Devonshire about raising potatoes and keeping bees?'; ends calling Plath 'O funny duchess! O blonde thing!'
back: Sylvia's Death, from Live or Die (Anne Sexton)

front: Poem: 17 retellings of Grimms' fairy tales; 'Rapunzel' opens 'a woman / who loves a woman / is forever young'
Author: drank at the Ritz-Carlton with Sylvia Plath and George Starbuck after writing seminars organized by Robert Lowell; mentored by W.D. Snodgrass
Poem: the speaker dreams of 'a city made of chains' and declares 'I'm no more a woman than Christ was a man'
Poem: about Icarus, ending as 'his sensible daddy goes straight to town'; parodied Yeats
back: To a Friend Whose Work Has Come to Triumph (Anne Sexton)
